mnemonics: B.A.D. Sets: A set is Bounded if All Distances between points are finite. B.A.D. = Bounded All Distances.
keyPoints: A set S in a metric space (X,d) is bounded if there exists a real number M > 0 and a point a in X such that d(x,a) ≤ M for all x in S.
Equivalently, a set is bounded if its diameter (sup{d(x,y) : x,y in S}) is finite.
In R^n with the standard Euclidean metric, a set is bounded if it can be contained within some ball of finite radius.
commonMistakes: Confusing a bounded set with a totally bounded set; total boundedness is a stronger condition.
Assuming that a closed and bounded set is always compact; this is only true in specific metric spaces like R^n with the standard metric.
Forgetting that the choice of the point 'a' in the definition is arbitrary; the existence of any such point and radius M is sufficient.
